P.description: Patients in various clinical settings, including those with diabetes and nursing home residents.
P.demographics: Includes persons of low literacy, non-English speaking patients, and the frail elderly. Specific demographics are not detailed in the text.
P.setting: Clinical settings, practice-based settings, nursing homes, and real-world clinical settings.

I.description: Various medical interventions and treatments being compared for effectiveness.

C.description: Alternative interventions

O.measurement: Various measures including patient-reported comorbidity, disease burden, and data from Minimum Data Set.

Confounders.adjustment_method: Propensity scoring, various types of modeling (including Bayesian approaches), practice-based evidence study (PBE) methodology

parsed.study_design: Review of symposium papers covering various study designs including primary data collection, analysis of databases (electronic health records, distributed networks), practice-based evidence studies.
parsed.sample_size: Not specified, as this is a review of multiple studies presented at a symposium.
parsed.causal_question: Does a specific medical intervention affect health outcomes in a heterogeneous patient population, compared to alternative interventions, after adjusting for patient, treatment, and outcome heterogeneity?

P.description: Individuals with type 2 diabetes and no prior history of kidney disease.
P.demographics: Age and sex were standardized for. Included non-Hispanic white (NHW), Asian (Asian Indian, Chinese, and Filipino), Hispanic, and non-Hispanic black (NHB) race/ethnicity.
P.setting: Outpatient health care organization

variables: Race/Ethnicity (Asian Indian, Chinese, Filipino, Hispanic, Non-Hispanic Black)
I.description: Race/ethnicity as a determinant of diabetic kidney disease prevalence.

C.description: Non-Hispanic White (NHW)

O.primary_outcome: Prevalence of proteinuric diabetic kidney disease (DKD)
O.measurement: Age- and sex-adjusted prevalence rates and odds ratios

Confounders.adjustment_method: Adjustment was done via calculation of sex-specific odds ratios.

parsed.causal_question: Does race/ethnicity affect the prevalence of proteinuric and nonproteinuric diabetic kidney disease in individuals with type 2 diabetes, compared to non-Hispanic whites, after adjusting for traditional DKD risk factors?

P.description: Male veterans undergoing outpatient colonoscopy
P.demographics: Mean age 60.6 years (SD 10.5), 93.8% male
P.setting: Veterans Affairs Medical Center, Indianapolis, Indiana (outpatient setting)

I.description: Body Mass Index, specifically a BMI of 30 kg/m2 or greater

C.description: Patients with a BMI less than 30 kg/m2

O.measurement: Categorized as adequate (excellent or good) or inadequate based on the Aronchick scale

Confounders.adjustment_method: Multivariable analysis

parsed.causal_question: Does a BMI of 30 kg/m2 or greater affect the likelihood of inadequate bowel preparation in male veterans undergoing outpatient colonoscopy, compared to a BMI less than 30 kg/m2, after adjusting for Charlson comorbidity score, tobacco use, narcotics use, hypertension, diabetes, and dementia?

entry.description: Body Mass Index, specifically a BMI of 30 kg/m2 or greater

entry.name: Outcome (O)

entry.measurement: Categorized as adequate (excellent or good) or inadequate based on the Aronchick scale

entry.name: Confounders

P.description: Patients undergoing percutaneous image-guided palliative cryoablation for painful musculoskeletal metastatic disease.
P.demographics: Not extensively reported, but included data on primary tumor type and anatomical data. Total of 50 patients (54 tumors ablated).
P.setting: Single center (University Hospitals Case Medical Center and Emory University Hospital)

I.description: Image-guided cryoablation performed using cryoprobes (13-17G) with two 10-5 minute freeze-thaw intervals.

C.description: No direct comparison group, but the study implies comparison to other pain management strategies (radiation, surgery, analgesic medications).
C.type: Implicitly, prior or alternative pain management strategies.

secondary_outcomes: Change in analgesic requirements (morphine equivalent dosages)
O.measurement: Pain scores measured using a visual analog scale (VAS). Analgesic requirements measured as morphine equivalent dosages.

Confounders.adjustment_method: Not explicitly stated, but an intent-to-treat analysis was performed.

parsed.causal_question: Does CT-guided cryoablation affect pain scores and analgesic requirements in patients with painful musculoskeletal metastatic disease, compared to their baseline, after adjusting for demographic, anatomical, and tumor-related factors?

fhir.causal_question: Does CT-guided cryoablation affect pain scores and analgesic requirements in patients with painful musculoskeletal metastatic disease, compared to their baseline, after adjusting for demographic, anatomical, and tumor-related factors?

P.description: Individuals with incident gout and matched controls from the UK general population.
P.demographics: Age 18-89 years (no further demographic details provided in text)
P.setting: Primary care (general practitioner) settings throughout the UK, using the Health Improvement Network (THIN) database.

variables: Diagnosis of gout (defined by READ code)
Urate-lowering therapy (ULT) prescription within 3 months of diagnosis (alternative definition)
I.description: Incident diagnosis of gout, identified through electronic medical records.

C.description: Individuals with no gout.

O.measurement: Mortality rates (deaths/1000 person-years) and Hazard Ratios (HRs)

Confounders.adjustment_method: Multivariable regression

parsed.sample_size: Data from over 10 million patients in the THIN database
parsed.causal_question: Does having a diagnosis of gout affect all-cause mortality in individuals aged 18-89 in the UK, compared to those without gout, after adjusting for potential confounders?

P.description: Patients with acute traumatic spinal cord injury (TSCI)
P.demographics: Age, sex, weight, race, education, and insurance status are reported. No specific age range or racial/ethnic breakdown is provided in the text.
P.setting: Regional, level I trauma center (Harborview Medical Center, University of Washington)

I.description: Exposure to methylprednisolone therapy

C.description: Patients who did not receive methylprednisolone

O.measurement: Rehabilitation discharge in American Spinal Injury Association (ASIA) motor scores (0–100) and Functional Independence Measure (FIM) motor scores (13–91). Infection and GI complications were measured as risk.

Confounders.adjustment_method: Multivariable linear regression

parsed.causal_question: Does methylprednisolone exposure affect motor function in patients with acute traumatic spinal cord injury, compared to no exposure, after adjusting for age, sex, weight, race, baseline motor score, surgical intervention, injury level, ASIA Impairment Scale grade, education, and insurance status?

entry.name: Population (P)

entry.demographics: Age, sex, weight, race, education, and insurance status are reported. No specific age range or racial/ethnic breakdown is provided in the text.
entry.setting: Regional, level I trauma center (Harborview Medical Center, University of Washington)

entry.name: Intervention/Exposure (I)
entry.description: Exposure to methylprednisolone therapy

entry.name: Comparison (C)
entry.description: Patients who did not receive methylprednisolone

entry.name: Outcome (O)
entry.description: Improvements in motor function
entry.measurement: Rehabilitation discharge in American Spinal Injury Association (ASIA) motor scores (0–100) and Functional Independence Measure (FIM) motor scores (13–91). Infection and GI complications were measured as risk.

entry.name: Confounders
entry.description: Multivariable linear regression

fhir.causal_question: Does methylprednisolone exposure affect motor function in patients with acute traumatic spinal cord injury, compared to no exposure, after adjusting for age, sex, weight, race, baseline motor score, surgical intervention, injury level, ASIA Impairment Scale grade, education, and insurance status?

P.description: Individuals from the Kaiser Permanente Hawaii health system.
P.demographics: Mean age 54.1 years (SD 17.8), 127 women and 97 men.
P.setting: Kaiser Permanente Hawaii health system, Honolulu, Hawaii

I.description: Presence or absence of a clinical diagnosis of thyroid disease.

C.description: Two control groups: a general Kaiser Permanente Hawaii population control group and an ophthalmology clinic control group.

O.measurement: Clinical diagnosis of uveitis determined through electronic medical record query and specialist review.

Confounders.adjustment_method: Logistic regression analysis

parsed.sample_size: 224 patients with uveitis, 896 patients in each control group
parsed.causal_question: Does a history of thyroid disease affect the odds of having uveitis in individuals from the Kaiser Permanente Hawaii health system, compared to those without thyroid disease, after adjusting for age, sex, race, smoking status, and autoimmune disease?

P.description: Adults undergoing myocardial perfusion imaging (MPI)
P.demographics: Adults (age >18 years)
P.setting: Large community teaching hospital in Detroit, Michigan

I.description: Use of either regadenoson or dipyridamole as a vasodilator agent during myocardial perfusion imaging.

C.description: Regadenoson vs. Dipyridamole

O.primary_outcome: Composite occurrence of any documented adverse event
secondary_outcomes: Individual components of the primary endpoint
Reason for termination of MPI examination
Use of interventional agent to treat adverse event
O.measurement: Data collected from electronic medical records; proportions and p-values reported.

variables: Not explicitly stated, but the study design implies consideration of baseline patient characteristics
Confounders.adjustment_method: Not explicitly stated, retrospective cohort design

parsed.causal_question: Does the use of regadenoson or dipyridamole affect the frequency of adverse events in adults undergoing myocardial perfusion imaging, compared to each other, after considering the setting of a large community teaching hospital?

P.description: Patients with symptomatic acute or chronic left iliocaval venous obstruction.
P.demographics: Median age 46 (range 15-86 years), 80% female (301/376)
P.setting: Two hospitals: University College Hospital of Galway, Ireland and CHU Nord, Marseille, France

variables: Left common iliac venous stent placement
I.description: Placement of a stent in the left common iliac vein, which may extend into the inferior vena cava.

C.description: Absence of stent placement or contralateral DVT prior to follow-up

secondary_outcomes: Primary patency of limbs after clot removal
O.measurement: Cumulative incidence of contralateral DVT calculated using Kaplan-Meier analysis; patency assessed at 3 years.

variables: Pre-operative contralateral internal iliac vein (IIV) thrombosis
Confounders.adjustment_method: Statistical analysis (p-values reported, suggesting multivariable analysis, though not explicitly stated)

parsed.causal_question: Does left common iliac venous stent placement affect the risk of contralateral iliac DVT, compared to not having a stent, after adjusting for acute DVT, pre-operative contralateral IIV thrombosis, pre-existing IVC filters, and anticoagulation non-compliance?

characteristic.description: Pre-operative contralateral internal iliac vein (IIV) thrombosis

entry.description: Statistical analysis (p-values reported, suggesting multivariable analysis, though not explicitly stated)

fhir.causal_question: Does left common iliac venous stent placement affect the risk of contralateral iliac DVT, compared to not having a stent, after adjusting for acute DVT, pre-operative contralateral IIV thrombosis, pre-existing IVC filters, and anticoagulation non-compliance?

P.description: Adults with type 1 or type 2 diabetes in a primary care setting.
P.demographics: Mean age 69.4 years (range 16-89), type 1 diabetes (n=1370), type 2 diabetes (n=12,585), sex not specified in detail.
P.setting: Primary care in the UK (based on hospital affiliations of authors)

variables: Townsend index score (quintiles of social deprivation)
I.description: Presence or absence of foot ulceration and level of social deprivation.

C.description: Individuals without foot ulceration, and those in the lowest quintile of deprivation.

secondary_outcomes: New foot ulceration (in those without a previous history)
O.measurement: Cox proportional hazard models were used to assess incidence of foot ulceration and mortality rates.

Confounders.adjustment_method: Multivariable Cox regression

parsed.causal_question: Does foot ulceration and social deprivation affect mortality in adults with type 1 or type 2 diabetes, compared to those without foot ulceration or lower social deprivation, after adjusting for age, sex, diabetes type, smoking status, hypertension, statin use, β-blocker use, metformin use, HbA1c levels and insulin use?

entry.setting: Primary care in the UK (based on hospital affiliations of authors)

fhir.causal_question: Does foot ulceration and social deprivation affect mortality in adults with type 1 or type 2 diabetes, compared to those without foot ulceration or lower social deprivation, after adjusting for age, sex, diabetes type, smoking status, hypertension, statin use, β-blocker use, metformin use, HbA1c levels and insulin use?

P.description: Patients presenting to the Emergency Department (ED) for acute ethanol intoxication.
P.demographics: Median age 37 years, 69% male
P.setting: 6 Emergency Departments (1 Level 1 trauma center, 2 urban university medical centers, 2 suburban community hospitals, 1 rural community hospital) in Minnesota, USA

I.description: Exposure to factors potentially influencing ED length of stay (LOS) in patients with acute ethanol intoxication.

C.description: Absence of parenteral sedation, no laboratory testing, arrival during other hours

O.measurement: Minutes (median LOS 348 min, range 43-1658)

variables: Frequent ED use for ethanol intoxication
Confounders.adjustment_method: Mixed-effects generalized linear model

parsed.causal_question: Does parenteral sedation, laboratory testing, or hour of arrival affect ED length of stay in patients with acute ethanol intoxication, compared to no sedation, no testing, or different arrival times, after adjusting for age, gender, ethanol concentration, psychiatric disposition, frequent ED use, CT use, and daily ED volume?

P.description: Patients with both cerebral infarcts (ischemic stroke) and Chagas disease admitted to rehabilitation hospitals.
P.demographics: Mean age 61 years old. Sex distribution not explicitly stated, but female sex was a factor in recurrence analysis.
P.setting: Rehabilitation hospitals across Brazil (public, open access, quaternary health institutions in 8 cities)

I.description: Presence of both ischemic stroke and confirmed Chagas disease (via serological tests)

C.description: Not explicitly stated, but implied to be patients without Chagas disease or those without a history of stroke.

O.measurement: Mortality was measured as frequency of death. Recurrence was assessed via follow-up and likely clinical evaluation/imaging (details not provided).

Confounders.adjustment_method: Cox regression analysis for mortality and logistic regression for recurrence

parsed.causal_question: Does having both ischemic stroke and Chagas disease affect mortality and stroke recurrence in this population, compared to those without these conditions, after adjusting for age, initial mRS, bladder dysfunction, diabetes mellitus, alcoholism, cognitive deficit, and cardioembolic etiology?

P.description: Patients admitted to a tertiary vascular unit
P.demographics: Not explicitly stated, but includes both male and female patients, with age being a factor in sarcopaenia prevalence.
P.setting: Tertiary vascular unit, Aberdeen Royal Infirmary, UK

I.description: Presence or absence of sarcopaenia, defined as skeletal muscle index of <41 cm2/m2 in female patients and non-obese males and <53 cm2/m2 in obese males.

C.description: Patients without sarcopaenia

O.measurement: Mortality ascertained from patient records, non-home discharge also from records.

Confounders.adjustment_method: Multivariate analysis

parsed.causal_question: Does sarcopaenia affect mortality and non-home discharge in vascular surgical patients, compared to those without sarcopaenia, after adjusting for age, sex, and diagnosis type?

P.description: Adult patients with uncontrolled type 2 diabetes receiving at least one oral antidiabetic agent.
P.demographics: Adults with type 2 diabetes; race/ethnicity (Hispanic vs. White); sex (male/female); BMI
P.setting: Large, accountable care organization (ACO) in the United States

I.description: Treatment intensification following an HbA1c level ≥8.0% within a 120-day window.

C.description: Patients who did not receive treatment intensification during the 120-day window.

O.measurement: HbA1C levels measured through electronic medical records and diabetes registry data.

variables: Number of pre-index oral antidiabetes agents
Physician specialty (internist vs. family medicine specialist)
Confounders.adjustment_method: Two-level hierarchical generalized linear models

parsed.sample_size: 547 patients met study selection criteria, 480 used for statistical analyses
parsed.causal_question: Does treatment intensification affect HbA1C control in patients with uncontrolled type 2 diabetes, compared to no treatment intensification, after adjusting for index HbA1C, BMI, pre-index oral antidiabetes agents, race/ethnicity, sex, and physician specialty?

entry.name: Population (P)
entry.description: Adult patients with uncontrolled type 2 diabetes receiving at least one oral antidiabetic agent.
entry.demographics: Adults with type 2 diabetes; race/ethnicity (Hispanic vs. White); sex (male/female); BMI
entry.setting: Large, accountable care organization (ACO) in the United States

entry.name: Intervention/Exposure (I)
entry.description: Treatment intensification following an HbA1c level ≥8.0% within a 120-day window.

entry.name: Comparison (C)
entry.description: Patients who did not receive treatment intensification during the 120-day window.

entry.name: Outcome (O)
entry.description: Achieving HbA1C quality performance measures (HbA1C <8.0%).
entry.measurement: HbA1C levels measured through electronic medical records and diabetes registry data.

entry.name: Confounders

characteristic.description: Number of pre-index oral antidiabetes agents
Physician specialty (internist vs. family medicine specialist)

entry.description: Two-level hierarchical generalized linear models

fhir.sample_size: 547 patients met study selection criteria, 480 used for statistical analyses
fhir.causal_question: Does treatment intensification affect HbA1C control in patients with uncontrolled type 2 diabetes, compared to no treatment intensification, after adjusting for index HbA1C, BMI, pre-index oral antidiabetes agents, race/ethnicity, sex, and physician specialty?

P.description: Patients undergoing ureteroscopy for upper tract stone disease.
P.demographics: Age (mean age not reported), sex (not reported), race (not reported), BMI, ASA score.
P.setting: Single institution (Vanderbilt University Medical Center) in Tennessee, USA.

variables: Preoperative opiate prescriptions (number, days prescribed, unique providers)
I.description: Opiate exposure, both before and after ureteroscopy.

C.description: Patients without preoperative opiate prescriptions, and patients not requiring additional prescriptions postoperatively.

O.primary_outcome: Additional opiate prescription filled within 30 days after ureteroscopy.
secondary_outcomes: Persistent opiate use more than 60 days postoperatively
O.measurement: Prescription data from a statewide prescribing database (TN CSMD).

Confounders.adjustment_method: Not explicitly stated in the provided text, but the study 'determined predictors' implying statistical adjustment.

parsed.causal_question: Does opiate exposure (preoperative prescriptions and postoperative prescriptions) affect the likelihood of additional opiate prescriptions within 30 days and persistent opiate use 60 days after ureteroscopy in kidney stone formers, after adjusting for demographic, surgical, and stone characteristics?

P.demographics: 95.4% male, 74% HIV co-infected. Age and race/ethnicity not reported.
P.setting: Quaternary care center (The Alfred Hospital and Monash University, Melbourne, Australia)

C.description: Patients who did or did not receive neurosyphilis treatment based on CSF findings.

O.primary_outcome: Concordance between CSF analysis and subsequent antibiotic strategy.
secondary_outcomes: Confirmation of neurosyphilis diagnosis (confirmed, supportive, not supportive)
Proportion of patients treated with neurosyphilis antibiotics despite lacking CSF findings
Proportion of patients not treated despite supportive CSF findings
O.measurement: Categorical classification of CSF findings and treatment decisions based on existing definitions and guidelines.

variables: Specific neurological manifestations (meningitis, cranial neuropathy, cognitive decline, etc.)
Confounders.adjustment_method: Not explicitly stated, but clinical presentation and indications for LP were reviewed to categorize cases.

P.description: Patients with chronic liver disease (CLD) or cirrhosis attending an outpatient hepatology clinic.
P.demographics: Median age 53 years, 53% with cirrhosis, 45% with HCV, 26% with NAFLD, 10% with alcohol-related liver disease. Sex and race/ethnicity are not reported.
P.setting: Outpatient hepatology clinic in Ontario, Canada

variables: Hierarchical algorithm incorporating laboratory and administrative codes
I.description: A hierarchical algorithm designed to define CLD and cirrhosis etiology using both laboratory data and administrative codes.

C.description: Gold standard liver disease etiology determined by an attending hepatologist.

O.primary_outcome: Diagnostic accuracy of the algorithm in defining etiology.
O.measurement: Calculated sensitivity, specificity, PPV, NPV, and kappa’s agreement.

Confounders.adjustment_method: Not explicitly mentioned in the provided text. The study focuses on validation rather than identifying risk factors.

parsed.study_design: Validation study using chart abstraction and linkage to administrative data.
parsed.causal_question: Does a hierarchical algorithm accurately define CLD and cirrhosis etiology in patients, compared to a gold standard determined by a hepatologist?

P.description: Patients with diabetic macular oedema (DMO) receiving anti-VEGF therapy.
P.demographics: 1964 patients, 2614 eyes. Male gender was a significant predictor. Age not reported.
P.setting: Tertiary referral centre (Moorfields Eye Hospital, London, UK)

variables: Intravitreal anti-vascular endothelial growth factor (anti-VEGF) injections
I.description: Treatment with intravitreal anti-VEGF injections for diabetic macular oedema.

C.description: Not explicitly stated, but implied as pre-treatment visual acuity or no treatment.

O.primary_outcome: Time to reach a favourable visual outcome of more than 70 Early Treatment Diabetic Retinopathy Study (ETDRS) letters.
O.measurement: Early Treatment Diabetic Retinopathy Study (ETDRS) letters, measured over a 48-month observation period.

Confounders.adjustment_method: Cox proportional-hazards modelling

parsed.causal_question: Does the number of anti-VEGF injections affect time to reach a visual acuity of 70 ETDRS letters or greater in patients with DMO, compared to baseline, after adjusting for male gender and baseline VA?

P.description: Patients undergoing left ventricular assist device (LVAD) implantation at a non-transplant center.
P.demographics: Average age 65.7 years, 80% male, 51% with ischemic etiology, 65% INTERMACS profile 1 or 2.
P.setting: Baylor Scott and White, The Heart Hospital, Plano, Texas, USA - a non-transplant center.

I.description: Implantation of a left ventricular assist device (LVAD) as destination therapy (DT).

C.description: Patients who are not implanted with an LVAD (implicitly, those receiving medical therapy alone or considered but declined for LVAD)

secondary_outcomes: Right ventricular failure requiring mechanical support
O.measurement: Kaplan-Meier method for survival analysis, assessed through electronic medical record review.

Confounders.adjustment_method: Multivariable Cox proportional hazards regression models

parsed.causal_question: Does LVAD implantation affect survival in patients ineligible for heart transplantation, compared to not receiving an LVAD, after adjusting for renal function, CPB time, and preoperative hemoglobin?
